1
 
Republic of Zambia 
Ministry of Education 
 
FRENCH LANGUAGE 
FORM 1 
TEACHING MODULE 
TERM 3 
 
Developed by the Curriculum Development Centre 
Lusaka 
2025

2
Authors  
 
1. Ms Annie Mathatha  
Curriculum Development Specialist - French  
 
 
 
 
(CDC) 
2. Mr Mukundwe Siame 
Lecturer - French, University of Zambia (UNZA) 
3. Mr Machinyise Elliot 
Lecturer - French,  David Livingstone College  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
of  Education (DALICE), Livingstone. 
4. Mr Micheal Manda  
Teacher - French,  Chipata Day Secondary   
 
 
 
 
School,  
 
 
 
Chipata. 
5. Mrs Agness M, Cheundwe Teacher - French,  Kombaniya Secondary School,  
 
 
 
 
Mansa. 
6. Ms Julien Kunda Matanda Teacher - French,  Milemu Secndary School, Ndola. 
7. Mr Joseph Muwika  
Teacher - French,  Matete Secondary School, Kitwe.

3
PREFACE 
 
The advent of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) heralds a new era of 
dynamic and practical learning experiences designed to equip learners with the 21st 
century skills. It marks a trans-formative step in improving education quality and 
relevance.  
 
The French Language Teaching Module has been designed in line with the 
Competence Based Education principles which seeks to link education to real life 
experiences. The General Competences provided in the 2023 Zambia Curriculum 
Framework are linked to the content of this module.  
 
The module aims to support French teachers in the implementation of the Competence 
Based Curriculum in the absence of approved text books. It therefore reflects the 
Ministry’s unwavering commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible for 
teachers. However, teachers are encouraged to supplement this module with their own 
research and innovations in order to address any gaps that may arise during 
implementation. 
I hope teachers will make good use of this resource.  
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

4
ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
The French Language Teaching Module has been developed through a consultative 
and participatory process. Many individuals, institutions and organisations were 
consulted and accorded an opportunity to make proposals about the content of this 
teaching module. I would, therefore, like to express my profound gratitude to 
individuals, institutions and organisations that provided the financial and technical 
input to the successful development of this teaching module. These include; teachers, 
ecturers from colleges of education and universities, subject associations, civil society 
organisations, and cooperating partners. 
Finally, I appreciate the commitment and hard work of the staff at the Directorate of 
Curriculum Development in ensuring the successful completion of the Teaching 
Module. 
 
Charles Ndakala, (Dr.) 
Director – Curriculum Development 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

6
UNIT: 11 
TOPIC 1.11.0: MY SCHOOL 
 
Overview 
This topic is essential for learners as it provides foundational vocabulary and 
expressions related to daily life in a school setting. By exploring classroom orders and 
instructions, learners will learn how to navigate and communicate effectively in a 
French speaking classroom environment. Understanding subjects and school time 
tables helps learners to build the ability to describe the academic routine which is vital 
for engaging in conversations on personal interest among the learners. This topic also 
serves as a practical introduction to using the French language in a real world context, 
fostering confidence and fluency in everyday interactions. This topic has three sub 
topics namely, Classroom Orders and Instruction, My School Subjects and School 
Timetable. 
General Competences. 
Communication 
(i) 
Learners will effectively share their thoughts and emotions and understand 
others. 
(ii) 
Learners will use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different 
situations  and take turns when conversing with others. 
Emotional Intelligence:  
(i) 
Learners will communicate and relate effectively with others. 
 
Hook 
Imagine a place where you learn new things, play with your friends and grow 
everyday. That place is your school. Let us talk about what makes your school special 
in French. 
Sub Topic 1.11.1: Classroom Orders and Instructions in French. 
Introduction

7
Classroom orders and instructions are essential for creating a productive and 
supportive learning environment.  A well ordered classroom minimizes distractions 
allowing learners to focus better on their studies. In this subtopic, learners will learn 
key phrases used by teachers and learners in a French speaking classroom such as 
common commands, instructions and questions that facilitate communication.  
Specific competence 1.11.1.1: Use classroom orders and instructions in French. 
Key Terms/ Words/ Vocabulary  
Le vocabulaire: 
Les ordres - Ouvre ton livre à la page 3! / Ferme ton livre ! /Lève ta main gauche !  
Regarde !/ Parle plus fort !/Sors !/ Assieds-toi !/ Entrez !/ Venez !/va a ta place! 
Levez-vous! Asseyez –vous! Ecoutez! Repetez!Sortez! Taissez-vous. 
La grammaire: 
Les verbes - Sortir, faire, parler, entrer, regarder, fermer, manger, retourner, venir, 
aller, dégager… 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Listening to a list of French classroom expressions and find their meaning 
in English (Sortez - Entrez - Taisez-vous…). 
 Give instructions in French accompanied by gestures. 
 Learners listen to a set of demonstrated instructions and respond appropriately. 
e.g. Entrez !/ Venez ! Va a ta place ! Levez-vous.! 
 
Activity 2: Discussing classroom expressions / commands /orders in French. 
Content Tips: 
  Revision on ‘Les adjectifs possessifs’.

8
 In groups, learners find the meaning of the different classroom instructions 
and commands. 
 
Activity 3: Dramatizing classroom expressions or orders (Levez-vous!, Asseyez-
vous!, Ecoutez!, Repetez! …). 
 Learners role play classroom orders. 
 
Activity 4: Reading classroom expressions and translating them from French to 
English and vice versa. 
 Learners read and translate classroom expressions from French to English and 
vice versa. 
 
Expected Standard: Classroom orders and instructions used in French correctly. 
Key Points /Recap:  
i. 
Common classroom instructions and orders. 
Sub Topic: 1.11.2 My School Subjects 
Introduction 
This sub topic will enable learners to learn names of different school subjects in 
French and be able to express preferences about their favorite and least favorite 
subjects. The sub-topic will also introduce how to ask questions about subjects and 
discuss the subjects learners study. 
Specific Competence 1.11.2.1 Identify different school subjects in French. 
Key Terms/words/vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire: 
Les matières: Le français,l’anglais,les mathématiques, le civisme, la géographie, 
l’histoire,l’éducation religieuse, l’informatique, les sciences etc… 
La grammaire

9
Les Verbes - aimer, préférer, avoir, prendre, étudier. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Listing different subjects offered in a school (le Français, les 
Mathématiques…). 
 Learners mention the subjects they know in secondary school. 
 Provide a simple passage on ‘Les matières’: e.g Le Français, l’anglais,les 
mathématiques, le civisme, la géographie, l’histoire, l’éducation religieuse, 
l’informatique, les sciences etc… 
 Learners identify and list the different subjects mentioned in the passage. 
 
Activity 2: Discussing school subjects (les matières préferées). 
 In groups, learners make sentences using verbs préférer and aimer. 
e.g J’aime le Civisme 
Nous préférons l’informatique. 
Il n’aime pas la Chimie. 
 
Content Tips:  
 Comparatif : ‘Que’ e.g. Je préfère les Mathématiques que la Biologie.  
 
 Superlatif : plus … que, très … que.  
La Science est plus facile que la Géographie.  
Le Français est très intéressant que l’Histoire. 
 
 Egalité : Aussi …que. e.g. La Physique est aussi difficile que la Chimie.

10
 Infériorité : Moins … que. e.g. L’Anglais est moins facile que les 
Mathématiques. 
 
 Parce que … E.g. Je préfère les Mathématiques parce que je veux être 
ingénieur.  
 
Activity 3: Writing a simple composition on one’s favourite subjects and explain 
why. 
 Learners write a guided composition on subjects they like and why. 
e.g: Your name, school, number and names of subjects, subjects preferred …  
 
Expected Standard: Different school subjects identified in French correctly. 
Key Points/ Recap ·  
i. 
Names of school subjects in French. 
ii. Le Comparatif, Le superlatif, L’égalité, L’infériorité. 
 
Sub- Topic 1.11.3 School Timetable  
 
Introduction 
Planning is an important aspect of every person’s daily activities. In this sub topic, 
learners will learn how to plan for their daily activities and come up with timetables in 
French. A school timetable is a summarised plan that describes learners activities. It 
smoothly guides learners to pursue their academic activities without any stress. This 
sub-topic discusses school and personal timetable and classroom objects. 
 
Specific Competence1.11.3.1 Draw up a school/personal timetable. 
 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary

11
Le vocabulaire: 
Nom de l’école, heure, matière, les jours de la semaine, matin, midi, après-
midi, recréation, classes … 
La grammaire: 
Les Verbes - avoir, étudier, réviser, être … 
 
Learning Activities 
            Activity 1: Discussing a school timetable (Remplir son emploi du temps avec 
les  
matières de son école.) 
 Provide and explain vocabulary that is related to a school 
timetable  
e.g les matières, classes, jour de la semaine, récréation, heure 
… 
 Present a sample of a school timetable written in French. 
 In groups, learners discuss the various French terms used on the 
given sample of a timetable. 
 
Activity 2: Formulating own study timetable (l’horaire personnel). 
 Provide learners with a blank timetable (a table with days of the 
week and times of the day). 
 Learners fill in the blank timetable given in French (Les 
mathématiques, le français…). 
 After filling in their personal timetables, learners share with 
partners or the class on what they do each day. 
 Learners interpret their timetable and practice constructing 
simple sentences in French using the vocabulary learned  
e.g le lundi, j’ai les Mathématiques à 9.00 heures. Le vendredi, 
j’ai l’anglais après la recréation … 
 
Activity 3: Writing a simple composition including the name of your school/ subjects 
you take and like.

12
 Provide a sample of guided composition in which a learner 
introduces him/herself and talk about their favourite subjects.  
e.g. Bonjour, je m’appelle Mulenga. J’ai quinze ans. J’habite à 
Lusaka. Je suis un élève à l’école David Kaunda. J’aime le 
français et la géographie. Ma matière préférée est … . 
  Learners write similar compositions using their own 
information. 
  
 
Expected Standard: School and personal timetables drawn correctly.  
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Features of a school/ personal study timetable. 
ii. Formulating a school/ personal timetable. 
 
Specific Competence 1.11.3.2 Describe different classroom objects. 
 
Key Terms/Words/ Vocabulary  
 
Le vocabulaire  
Les objets de classe: Un stylo,  livre, cahier, sac, un tableau, marqueur, une 
chaise, gomme, règle, calculatrice, un banc, une table, un frottoir craie, 
crayon, ordinateur, dictionnaire, atlas, papier, poubelle. 
Les expressions : Voici, voilà, il y a, pouvez –vous me passer, prenez votre/ 
vos…, j’ai / je n’ai pas de …, c’est pour écrire/ lire/ effacer, qu’est-ce que 
c’est ? c’est le/la …, donnez-moi le/la…Qu’est-ce que c’est? C’est … . 
 
La grammaire: Les adjectifs démonstratifs …  
Les Verbes : Mettre, prendre, donner… 
 
Learning Activities

13
            Activity 1: Listing different classroom objects (la table, le tableau…) 
 Show real items or images and say their names in French e.g un 
stylo, un crayon.  
 Each learner picks an object from their desk and says its name 
in French 
 Learners play a game (cherchez et trouvez une gomme …). 
 Learners draw the classroom items and label them in French (la 
table, le stylo, le banc, la gomme…). 
 Provide a short passage about classroom objects. 
 Learners answer questions from the passage. 
 
 
Activity 2: Dramatizing the named classroom objects using interrogative 
 
expressions (Qu’est-ce que c’est?  C’est …) 
 Learners dramatize the names of classroom objects. 
e.g.  
 
Qu’est – ce que c’est ?  
C’est le stylo. 
Content Tips:  
 Explain ‘les adjectifs démonstratifs’: ce, cet, cette, ces (this, these). 
e.g ce livre est bleu, cet élève est intelligent, cette table est sale, ces stylos sont 
rouges. 
Learners identify the correct ‘adjectif démonstratif’ e.g 
1. … stylo 
2. … ordinateur 
3. … crayons 
4.  ... table 
 
Expected Standard: Different classroom objects described correctly. 
 Assessment  
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity.

14
 Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the 
specific competence.  
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative 
assessment given at the end of the each topic. 
  The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed 
questions…) 
 
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Classroom objects in French. 
ii. Les adjectifs démonstratifs 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Tempo 1, Equipe

15
 
UNIT 12 
TOPIC 1.12.0 DAILY ACTIVITIES 
Overview 
Expressing daily activities is a universal concept that is relevant and relatable to all 
learners, regardless of their cultural background. This topic lays a foundation for 
everyday communication and helps learners to gain confidence in daily conversations, 
whether discussing their morning routines or leisure activities. It will enable learners 
to use key verbs and vocabulary while understanding the rhythm of daily life in 
French-speaking cultures. Thus, this topic will help learners connect the language to 
their real-life experiences and promote a more natural and practical use of French. 
The topic includes one subtopic: Telling Time in French. 
General Competences 
Communication 
(i) 
Develop basic conversational skills to clearly and confidently describe daily 
routines in French. 
(ii) 
Engage in basic dialogues, asking and answering questions about daily 
routines. 
 
Emotional Intelligence 
i. 
Recognise and respond to emotions related to daily activities.  
ii. Express personal feelings and emotions. 
iii. Show empathy towards others’ daily routines. 
Critical Thinking: 
(i) 
Compare and analyse different daily routines and habits among peers. 
(ii) 
Reflect on how daily habits can influence language use and social interactions. 
Hook

16
Show a series of pictures representing different daily activities (e.g. waking up, eating 
breakfast, going to work/school, exercising …). Ask students to identify these 
activities and arrange them in the order of a typical day. 
Sub-Topic 1.12.1 Telling Time 
Introduction  
Understanding how to tell time in French is a fundamental skill for learners. Time is 
an essential aspect of daily life, and knowing how to express it accurately enables 
learners to engage in a variety of everyday activities, from scheduling appointments to 
discussing plans and routines. In French, telling time differs slightly from English, 
particularly in expressing hours and minutes, as well as in the formal and informal 
structures used in conversations about time. This subtopic will help students not only 
learn how to read a clock in French but also understand the rhythm and structure of 
French time expressions. 
Specific Competence 1.12.1.1 Tell Time in French. 
Key Terms/Words/Vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire 
 Lundi/mardi … janvier, février … le jour, la journée, le matin, l’après-midi, le soir, la 
nuit, le jour, l’heure, minute, seconde, la soirée, le mois, la semaine, l’année, l’an, le 
temps, en retard, agenda, programme, la montre/pendule …  
Les expressions - faire la vaisselle, faire du sport, se brosser les dents, à quelle heure, 
le matin, l’après-midi, le soir, la nuit, tous les jours, une fois par semaine ...           
La grammaire : le présent, le futur proche. 
Les verbes - prendre, dire, faire, aller, raconter, suivre, narrer, retarder, se dépêcher, 
se doucher, habiller, se laver, se lever manger, boire, se coucher, lire, regarder, 
préparer, étudier.

17
Learning Activities  
Activity 1: Listening to a dialogue or conversation in which people ask and tell time 
in French (Quelle heure est-il? Il est…) 
 Provide a dialogue or conversation in which people ask and tell time. 
 Learners identify expressions used to ask and tell time e.g Quelle heure est-il? 
Il est quelle heure ? Vous avez l’heure, s’il vous plaît ? … 
 Explain the expressions used to ask and tell time in French. 
Activity 2: Asking and telling time in French. 
 Provide different expressions used to ask and tell time in French. 
Quelle heure est-il ? … Il est 7 heures 30.  
Il est quelle heure ? Il est 12 heures.  
Vous avez l’heure, s’il vous plaît ? Oui, il est 16 heures .... 
 Use visual aid like a clock or digital timer showing different times. Read the 
time aloud and ask learners to repeat after you. 
 Use a large paper clock with moveable hands. Call each learner to position the 
clock hands to match the time given. 
 Explain expressions to indicate half hour and quarter hour in French (… et 
demi, … et quart ...) e.g. je mange à douze heures et demi, Le bus arrive à huit 
heures et quart. 
Activity 3: Reading a passage on a bus schedule indicating departure and arrival time. 
 Provide a passage on a bus schedule indicating departure or arrival time. 
 Learners read the passage and answer questions relating to departure or arrival 
times. 
e.g. A quelle heure le bus arrive à Kitwe? Le bus arrive à Kitwe à 14 heures 
30.

18
Activity 4: Writing how to ask and tell time in French (Quelle heure est-il?  Il est 7 
heures 30) 
 Provide a written dialogue on asking and telling time with blank spaces e.g. 
Masauso: Quelle heure est-il? 
Namakao: .…. dix heures. 
Masauso: C’est …. heure la géographie? 
Namakao: La géographie est à ………..heures. 
 Learners complete the dialogue on asking and telling time in French.  
Expected Standard: Time expressed in French correctly. 
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Expressions used to ask and tell time in French. 
Specific Competence 1.12.1.2: Narrate daily activities in French. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Narrating daily activities in chronological order in reference to different 
times of the day (à six heures, je me lave, à sept heures, je prends le petit déjeuner). 
 Provide a passage of a person narrating their daily activities.   
 Learners identify the activities of the person in the passage and what time they 
are performed.  
 In groups, learners narrate their daily activities with the time they are 
performed. 
Activity 2: Making a diary in French

19
 Provide a written dialogue on daily routines in French. 
 Learners role-play the dialogue in French.  
Nkisu: “À quelle heure tu prends ton petit déjeuner?”  
Wana: Je prends mon petit déjeuner à huit heures et demi. 
Nkisu: “Qu’est-ce que tu fais après l’école?” 
Wana: Je fais la vaisselle. 
 Provide a cloze passage on daily routine accompanied with possible answers. 
Answers:  douze, vais, je, à, 
e.g. Le matin, je me lève …. 6h45. A 8h00, je … à l’école, et … mange mon 
déjeuner à ….heures.  
 Learners complete the cloze passage on daily routine. 
Activity 3: Writing on one’s daily activities. 
 Learners write a paragraph on their daily activities in a chronological order. 
Expected Standard: Daily activities narrated in French accordingly. 
Key Points/Recap 
i. 
Expressions used to talk about daily activities in French 
ii. Expressions used to ask and tell time in French 
Specific Competence 1.12.1.3 Identify days of the week and months of the year in 
French. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Naming the days of the week (Lundi, mardi…) and months of the year 
(janvier, février…)

20
 Provide a list of days of the week in French and their pronunciation. 
 Learners write down the days of the week and pronounce them in French 
correctly.  
 Provide a list of months of the year in French and their pronunciation.  
 Learners write down the months of the year and pronounce them in French 
correctly.  
Activity 2: Making a calendar 
 Using a real calender, explain the features of a calender (la date, jour de la 
semaine, les mois de l’année). 
 Guide learners on how to make a calender   
e.g 
Step 1: write a year on top e.g. 2025. 
Step 2: write a month on top e.g. Janvier, février … 
Step 3: Fill in the days of the week e.g. lundi, mardi, mercredi, jeudi… 
Step 4: Fill in the dates e.g. 1, 2, 3, 4… 
 Learners make a calendar for the year.  
Expected Standard: Days of the week and months of the year identified in French 
correctly. 
Assessment 
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity. 
  Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve 
the specific competence. 
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative 
assessment given at the end of each topic  
 The assessment activities shall include project based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open ended and closed 
questions…)

21
Key Points/ Recap 
i. 
Vocabulary for daily activities in French. 
ii. Telling time. 
iii. Narration of daily activities. 
iv. Days of the week and months of the year in French. 
v. Making a calendar. 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Tempo 1/ Contact 1/Latitude 1/Visual 
Aids.

22
 
 
UNIT  13 
TOPIC 1 .13.0 THE PAST 
  
Overview  
The past refers to a time that has already happened, before the present moment. It 
could be recent (yesterday, last week) or distant (years ago). The past usually means 
past tense and it is used to talk about actions or events that have already happened.  In 
French, learners start by learning passé compose which is the most commonly used 
past tense in everyday conversations. It is used to talk about completed actions in the 
past. This topic introduces learners to time expressions often used with the past. It 
lays a foundation for expressing oneself more fully in French and leads to future 
learning of other past tenses like imparfait. 
 
General Competences 
Communication 
i. 
Learners will develop communication skills by learning how to express past 
events share personal experiences and engage in conversations using the passé 
composé both in their spoken and written French. 
Critical thinking 
(i) 
Learners will analyse sentence structures, choose between the correct helping 
verbs  (avoir or être) and apply rules to various verbs and contexts. 
Creativity and Innovation:  
(i) 
Learners will create short stories about past experiences, encouraging them to 
use  
their imagination. 
Hook  
Show a picture of an action and say ‘Aujourd’hui, je mange. Hier, j’ai mangé’. Ask 
the learners to guess the meaning of the second sentence. 
 
Sub-Topic  1.13.1 The Past Actions 
 
Introduction

23
Understanding past actions and the tenses used to describe them is essential as it 
allows learners to share their experiences, tell stories and connect events that shape 
their day to day lives. In this sub topic, learners will learn how to use le passé 
composé and l’imparfait to talk about completed actions in the past. 
 
Specific Competence 1.13.1.1  Describe past actions in French. 
 
Key Terms /Words/ Vocabulary 
Les expressions de temps 
Hier (yesterday), La semaine dernière (last week), Il y a… (… ago), Ce matin (this 
morning), Une fois (once) 
Grammaire  
Le passé composé : je suis + (participe passé) /-il est + (participe passé) / j’ai + 
(participe passé) l’opposition entre présent et passé composé  
L’auxiliaire (helping verb) – Either avoir or être, used to form the past tense. 
Le participe passé – The past participle of the main verb (e.g. mangé, fini, pris). 
Les verbes avec être – Verbs that use être in passé composé (often movement verbs, 
like aller, venir, etc.), including reflexive verbs. 
L’accord du participe passé – Agreement of the past participle with the subject 
(when using être or with direct objects). 
L’imparfait - J’étais malade hier/ Nous étions en France la semaine passée. 
Learning Activities  
 
Activity 1: Listening to a passage on past happenings (je suis allé hier, j ai mangé)

24
 Play an audio or read a passage in which le passé composé has been used for 
the learners to understand the structure and its usage. 
 Guide learners in identifying the structure (le passé composé) from the 
passage e.g. Hier, je suis allé au marché. J’ai acheté une pomme. 
 In groups, learners identify the structure (le passé composé) used from the 
passage given. 
 
Activity 2: Composing le passé compose. 
 Explain the formation of le passé compose.   
Content tips: 
 
Le passé composé (past tense) is used to talk about completed actions. 
 L’auxiliaire (helping verb) – Either avoir or être, used to form the past tense. 
 Conjugaison du verbe avoir et être au temps présent. 
 Formation du participe passé des verbes réguliers (er) 
 When forming the participe passé of -er verbs, remove -er and add é e.g manger - 
mangé. Sujet + avoir + (participe passé). 
Chanter : Elle a chanté. 
Sujet+ être + (participe passé). 
Aller : Je suis allé à l’église. 
 Learners complete sentences given with the correct form of the verbs in 
brackets using le passé compose. 
e.g. Il ... (achêter) la chemise. Il a achêté la chemise. 
 
Activity 3: Distinguishing the verbs that use être and avoir 
Content tips 
Explain the following: 
 
Le passé composé avec être

25
i. 
some verbs have être as the auxiliary verb instead of avoir e.g. A group of 
common verbs mostly connected with movement and change. Some of them fall 
into pair of opposites which can help learners to learn. 
aller (to go) - venir (to come) 
arriver (to arrive) - partir (to leave) 
entrer (to go in ) - sortir (to go out) 
monter (to go up) - decendre (to go down) 
naitre (to be born) - mourrrir (to die) 
devenir (to become) - rentrer (to go back) - rester (to stay) 
retourner (to return) - revenir (to come back) - tomber (to fall). 
 
ii. All reflexive verbs use être as auxiliary verb. 
e.g. se laver, se lever. 
Except from the verbs listed above, the rest use avoir as auxiliary verb. 
 Provide le participe passé of verbs that use the auxiliary être. 
Content tips 
Explain the following: 
 L’accord du participe passé en genre et en nombre – Agreement of the past 
participle with the subject when using the ausxiliary être. 
e.g. il est parti /elle est partie, ils sont allés /elles sont allées. 
 Learners transform the given sentences from present to le passé composé 
using être. 
e.g. Elle va au marché - Elle est allée au marché. 
 Nous entrons dans la salle - Nous sommes entrées /entres dans la salle. 
 Learners complete the passage using the correct auxiliary verb and le participe 
passé. 
e.g. Hier, je …. (aller)à l’école. Je ….. (arriver) à 7.30 heures. Nous ….(étudier) le 
français avec mes amis. Apres classe, je ….. (rentrer) a la maison. Je …. (manger) 
mon déjeuner à 13.00 heures.

26
Activity 4: Role playing past actions using être/avoir + participe  passé ( passé  
composé) 
 In pairs learners talk about what they did during the weekend. 
 
Activity 5: Reading a passage on past happenings. 
 Provide a short passage on past events. 
 Learners respond to the questions based on the passage. 
 
Activity 6: writing a story on past events using pictures.  
 Provide picture stories. 
 Learners narrate a story in the past using the given pictures. 
 
Activity 7: Composing a story from past events.  
Content Tips 
 Introduce expressions used to narrate a story in the past. 
e.g. Hier (yesterday), La semaine dernière (last week), Il y a… (… ago), Ce matin (this 
morning), Quand J’étais petit(e), J’étais calme. 
 Explain l’imparfait: 
 L’Imparfait is sometimes called the past continuous tense. It is not made up of 
two parts like le passé composé, but it is just one word made of a stem and an 
ending. 
 The stem is the nous form of the present tense of the verb minus the -ons.  
 Formation of l’imparfait 
Verbe 
Nous form 
Stem  
L’imparfait 
Regarder 
Nous regardons 
regard 
regardions 
Prendre 
Nous prenons 
Pren 
Prenions  
 
Note: the endings are the same for all the verbs. 
Je - ais  
 
Nous - ions   
Tu - ais 
 
Vous - iez

27
Il /Elle/On - ait 
Ils /Elles - aient 
 
 Explain how to write an imaginative story from the past events. 
 Learners write an imaginative story from past events using le passé composé 
and l’imparfait. 
 
Expected Standard:  Past actions described in French correctly. 
Assessment. 
 Note that assessment will be done during and after each learning activity. 
 Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the 
specific competence. 
 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative 
assessment given at end of the topic. 
 The assessment activities shall include project-based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open-ended and closed 
questions…)  
Key Points / Recap 
i. 
Le passé composé,. 
ii. L’auxiliaire (helping verb) – Either avoir or être, used to form the past tense. 
iii. Le participe passé – mangé, fini, pris.. 
iv. Les verbes avec être – Verbs that use être in passé composé (often movement 
verbs, like aller, venir ...), reflexive verbs. 
v. L’accord du participe passé  
vi. L’imparfait 
vii. Les expressions de temps en Français

28
UNIT 14 
WEATHER 1.14.0 
 
Overview:  
The term weather encompasses a set of atmospheric conditions like temperature, rain, 
wind and sun that are observed at specific time in a particular place. The topic is 
essential for learners as it provides practical vocabulary and expressions useful in 
daily life. In French, discussing the weather not only helps the learners build their 
conversational skills but also enables them to engage in everyday interactions, 
whether making small talk with friends, checking the forecast, or planning activities. 
Understanding the weather introduces important grammar structures that help learners 
to practice both present tense and adjectives. This topic has three sub topics Weather 
conditions, Clothes and Activities and Factors affecting Weather. 
General Competences 
Communication 
(i) 
Learners will engage in basic conversations about the weather, providing an 
excellent  
opportunity to practice their listening and speaking skills.  
Critical Thinking 
(i) 
Learners will make observations, comparisons and predictions as they discuss 
how the  
weather impacts their daily lives or how it changes with the seasons. 
Analytical thinking 
i. 
Learners will identify and interpret patterns in both the language and the world 
around them.   
Hook 
Show a picture depicting a cold weather and say ‘aujourd’hui, il fait trés froid.’ask the 
learners what the weather is like.

29
Key-Terms/words/vocabulary 
Le vocabulaire  
Froid, chaud, pluie, vent, nuages, soleil, le printemps, l’été, l’automne, l’hiver, 
parapluie, feu inondation, fumée, poussière neige 
Les vêtements - manteau/tricot/gants/chapeau/veste/… 
Les activités - pendant la saison des pluies … cultiver, / la saison sèche … pêcher/ la 
saison froide …récolter/ moissonner.  
Les expressions  
Quel temps fait-il aujourd’hui /Il fait quel temps ? Quelle est la température 
d’aujourd’hui ?  
Il fait(très) beau/mauvais/ Il y a du soleil/ Il fait mauvais/beau temps /Il pleut/il y a du 
vent/ Il y a une tempête /des éclairs/ Il fait (très) chaud/frais/froid/ Il fait Cbrouillard 
/Il pleut/neige/il fait + ...(nombre) + degrés Celsius/ il y a du soleil  
La grammaire : le passé composé. 
Les Verbes: Cultiver, défricher, labourer, récolter, pêcher, pleuvoir, briller, sécher, 
brûler, couper, jeter. 
Sub- Topic1.14.1 Weather Conditions 
Introduction 
Weather conditions refers to the temporary and local state of the atmosphere at a 
specific place and time, including factors such as: temperature, rain, clouds, wind etc. 
This sub topic is essential for learners as it equips them with the basic vocabulary and 
expressions needed to describe the various weather conditions they encounter daily. 
The subtopic will also enhance the learner’s comprehension skills and enable them to 
discuss and respond appropriately in social situations. Furthermore, it will provide 
them with opportunities to practice adjectives and verbs in context, helping them to 
construct simple sentences.  
Specific Competences 1.14.1.1 Describe what the weather is like in French.

30
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Identify the expressions used to talk about weather from a given dialogue 
(il fait beau, froid …) 
 
 Provide a dialogue (audio) about weather.  
e.g 
 
Michelo:  
Salut! Quel temps fait-il aujourd’hui ? 
Mutale: 
 Il fait beau et chaud. 
Michelo: 
Ah, super! Hier il faisait très froid. 
Mutale: 
 Oui, il y a du soleil aujourd’hui. 
 
 Guide the learners to read the dialogue and explain the expressions used to 
talk about weather. 
 In pairs, learners role-play the dialogue on weather. 
 
Activity 2-Discussing what the weather is like on a particular day. 
 Introduce more expressions to talk about weather. 
e.g. Il pleut, il fait du vent. 
 In groups, learners describe the weather of a particular day. 
 
Activity 3: Display a chart showing different types of weather conditions. 
 Provide a chart showing different types of weather conditions. 
 Learners construct sentences on the different weather conditions displayed on 
the chart. 
 
Activity 4: Reading a passage on different types of weather conditions and answer 
questions based on the passage. 
 Provide a passage on different types of weather conditions. 
 Learners answer questions based on the passage.

31
Activity 5: Writing different dictated expressions used to talk about weather in 
French. 
 Dictate different expressions used to talk about weather in French. 
 Learners write the dictated expressions. 
 
Expected standard: Weather described in French correctly. 
Key points /Recap:  
i. 
Expressions used to talk about weather in French. 
Specific- Competence 1.14.12 Identify various seasons in French. 
Learning  Activities 
Activity 1: Discussing the different seasons in Zambia (saison sèche, saison de 
pluie.). 
 Learners name different seasons in Zambia. 
 Explain the different seasons in Zambia (saison sèche, saison de pluie …) 
 Learners identify months of the year for each season in French. 
e.g. saison de pluie - octobre, novembre, décembre, janvier, février, mars, avril. 
 
Activity 2: Describing the different season in French (local/foreign) printemps, hiver, 
été, automne) 
 Introduce and explain the seasons in French 
e.g. Printemps, l’hiver, l’été, l’automne) 
 Learners read and pronounce the seasons correctly. 
 Learners watch a video / images of different seasons in French. 
 Learners match the given images to the correct seasons in French. 
 
Expected standard: Seasons identified correctly. 
Key points/Recap

32
i. 
Seasons in Zambia. 
ii. Seasons in France. 
Sub-Topic 1.14.2 Clothes and Activities 
Introduction:   
Weather plays an important role in our daily lives. It influences our activities, mood, 
and clothes. Depending on the season, we adapt our clothes and activities to feel 
comfortable and enjoy the weather. 
Specific Competence 1.14.2.1 Describe activities and clothes associated with seasons 
in French. 
Key Terms/words/vocabulary. 
Le vocabulaire 
T-shirt, tricot, chemise, pantalon, shorts, jupe, chaussures, veste, écharpe, faire du 
vélo, pique-nique, faire du jardinage, cultiver, le printemps, l’hiver, l’été … . 
Learning Activities: 
Activity 1: Listening to a dialogue or conversation in which people are discussing 
clothes associated with the different seasons (froide – le tricot, sèche - culotte, T-
shirt). 
 Provide a passage or dialogue on different seasons and the type of clothes to 
wear. 
e.g. 
Dialogue on Weather 
Wana : Salut Wanji ! Il fait très froid aujourd’hui, non ? 
Wanji : Oui, c’est la saison froide ! Moi, je mets un manteau et un tricot. 
Wana : Moi aussi ! Et j’adore boire du chocolat chaud quand il fait froid. 
Wanji : Et toi, qu’est-ce que tu mets dans la saison sèche ? 
Wana : Je mets un t-shirt, une jupe ou un short, et des sandales. Et toi ? 
Wanji : Moi, je mets une culotte et des lunettes de soleil. J’aime aller à la mer !

33
Wana : Et la saison de pluie, quand il pleut ? 
Wanji : Je mets un imperméable et des baskets. Et toi ? 
Wana : Je porte un manteau de pluie et des bottes et j’apporte aussi un parapluie. 
Wanji: ok. Au revoir! 
Wana : A bientôt! 
  
 Learners identify and mention each type of season and clothes to wear. 
 Learners match the different clothes to the correct seasons in French. 
 
Activity 2: Discussing activities associated with different seasons (froide- récolter, 
pluie-cultiver). 
 Learners discuss activities done in each season. 
e.g: froide-récolter, pluie-cultiver. 
Activity 3: Reading a passage on clothes and activities associated with different 
seasons (make note summaries) 
 Provide a passage on clothes and activities associated with different seasons.  
 In pairs, learners read the passage provided and make summary notes. 
 Learners write a summary on clothes and activities associated with different 
seasons. 
  
Expected standard: Activities and clothes for specific seasons described in French 
correctly. 
Key Points/Recap  
i. 
Activities and clothes associated with seasons in French.  
ii. Expressions used to describe seasons in French. 
 
Sub - Topic1.14.3. Factors affecting Weather  
Introduction

34
Weather is a complex phenomenon that is influenced by many factors such as 
atmospheric conditions, geographic factors and human activities. Understanding the 
factors that influence the weather is essential for predicting weather conditions and 
marking informed decisions to protect people and the world we live in. 
Specific Competence.1.14.3.1 Discuss Factors Affecting Weather in Zambia  
Key Terms /Words /Vocabulary  
Le vocabulaire : 
Le temps, températures, vent, humidité, changement climatique, la météo, sombre. 
Learning Activities  
Activity 1: Identifying factors affecting weather from a given passage (temperature, 
les vents) 
 Provide a passage on factors affecting weather.  
e.g. La météo change chaque jour. Il fait chaud quand le soleil brille. Il fait froid 
quand il y a du vent. Les nuages cachent le soleil. Le ciel est sombre quand il pleu 
beaucoup. 
 Learners read and identify factors affecting weather. 
e.g. le soleil, la température, le vent, les nuages ….  
 
Activity 2: Discussing factors affecting weather in Zambia.  
 Explain other factors affecting weather in Zambia. 
e.g. déforestation, changement climatique, sécheresse ... 
 Give summarized notes in French on factors affecting climate change in 
Zambia.  
 
Expected standard: Factors affecting weather discussed in French correctly. 
Assessment.

35

 Provide assessment for each learning activity which is meant to achieve the

 The expected standard will be judged by the formative assessment for the 
learning activities from all the sub-topics taught and the summative 
assessment given at end of the topic. 
 The assessment activities shall include project-based tasks, practical 
demonstration, written assessment (quizzes, open-ended and closed 
questions…)  
Key/Point/Recap.  
i. 
Expressions to talk about weather in French 
ii. Clothes and activities to suite the weather  
iii. Factors affecting weather in Zambia
